title: Not Imaginary: The Deterrence Gap is Real and America Needs Low-Yield Nukes
gist: A former director of U.S. Nuclear Deterrence Policy who co-authored the 2018 and 2022 Nuclear Posture Reviews rebuts Jay Tilden's claim that the low-yield nuclear deterrence gap is 'imaginary,' arguing that Russia's and China's theater nuclear advantage is real, that multiple independent reviews and Congress reached the same conclusion, and that former officials have an obligation to speak out.

insights.claim: Russia and China are fielding diverse lower-yield nuclear forces to support coercive escalation strategies, giving Russia a significant advantage over the U.S. and allies in theater-range systems in both numbers and diversity.
insights.speaker: Paul Amato
insights.so_what: Underpins the bipartisan push to fund U.S. theater-nuclear modernization, a multi-decade procurement driver.
insights.quote: Russia retains a significant advantage over the United States and its allies in such theater range systems, both in numbers and in diversity.
insights.locator: In this respect, Russia retains a significant advantage

insights.claim: Putin boasted at the June 2026 St. Petersburg International Economic Forum that Europeans are 'more or less defenseless' against Russian tactical nuclear weapons.
insights.speaker: Paul Amato
insights.so_what: A current, on-the-record Russian signaling of theater-nuclear leverage that strengthens the case for Western response options.
insights.quote: Europeans are “more or less defenseless” against these capabilities.

insights.claim: The 2018 Nuclear Posture Review concluded the president lacked adequate options to restore deterrence short of an all-out strategic exchange if deterrence failed in a regional conflict, prompting the W76-2 warhead and the nuclear-armed sea-launched cruise missile.
insights.speaker: Paul Amato
insights.so_what: Documents the formal origin of current U.S. low-yield programs that defense budgets continue to fund.
insights.locator: From this, the Nuclear Posture Review concluded

insights.claim: Credible U.S. theater nuclear systems should be survivable in hours not days, forward-deployed in both Europe and Asia, offer a range of yields and fusing options, penetrate adversary defenses even in limited strikes, and be deliverable on operationally relevant timelines.
insights.speaker: Paul Amato
insights.so_what: A concrete requirements checklist that shapes which next-generation theater systems get built.

insights.claim: U.S. and allied dual-capable aircraft in Europe are vulnerable: in a conflict their bases and weapons-storage sites could be attrited conventionally, and many would be diverted to conventional missions before any nuclear threshold is reached.
insights.speaker: Paul Amato
insights.so_what: The fragility of the dual-capable aircraft backbone is the operational argument for survivable alternatives like SLCM-N.
insights.quote: there is no telling how many dual-capable aircraft – and their crews – will have been attrited.
insights.locator: Should conflict arise, say because of a Russian incursion

insights.claim: The Biden administration's 2022 Nuclear Posture Review identified the same deterrence challenge as 2018 and retained the W76-2 as 'an important means to deter limited nuclear use' even while cancelling the nuclear-armed sea-launched cruise missile.
insights.speaker: Paul Amato
insights.so_what: Bipartisan continuity signals durable demand for theater-nuclear capability across administrations.
insights.quote: currently provides an important means to deter limited nuclear use.
insights.locator: It then chose to retain the W76-2

insights.claim: Congress in 2024 overruled the Biden cancellation and directed establishment of a nuclear-armed sea-launched cruise missile program of record, which is ongoing.
insights.speaker: Paul Amato
insights.so_what: A live, congressionally mandated program creates a real procurement pipeline for SLCM-N contractors.
insights.quote: Congress in 2024 overruled the Biden Administration’s cancellation decision, directing establishment of a nuclear-armed sea-launched cruise missile program of record.
insights.locator: Second, and more important to refuting Tilden’s point

insights.claim: The 2023 bipartisan Strategic Posture Commission independently recommended urgently modifying U.S. theater nuclear posture to deter or counter Chinese or Russian limited nuclear use.
insights.speaker: Paul Amato
insights.so_what: A third independent body reaching the same conclusion reinforces the political durability of theater-nuclear funding.
insights.quote: a range of militarily effective nuclear response options to deter or counter Chinese or Russian limited nuclear use in theater.
insights.locator: The 2023 bipartisan Strategic Posture Commission report

insights.claim: Current plans to modernize and sustain the U.S. nuclear deterrent are projected by the CBO to cost nearly $1 trillion over the next decade.
insights.speaker: Paul Amato
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of the U.S. nuclear modernization spend that defense and industrial-base investors should track.
insights.quote: projected by the Congressional Budget Office to cost nearly $1 trillion over the next decade.
insights.locator: Second, current plans to modernize and sustain

insights.claim: Amato argues former officials have an obligation to speak publicly about deterrence gaps, citing Churchill's 1930s warnings about German rearmament as vindicated rather than reckless.
insights.speaker: Paul Amato
insights.so_what: Frames the expert-advocacy ecosystem that sustains political momentum for nuclear procurement.
insights.locator: Winston Churchill provides perhaps the most famous example
